questions.context: Perceptual constancy is the tendency of the visual system to perceive objects as stable in size, shape, and color despite continual changes in the image cast upon the retina. Every time an object moves or the light shifts, the raw signal reaching the eye is altered, yet the world appears reassuringly fixed. This stability is not given by the eye itself but is constructed by the brain from incomplete information. This construction depends on the brain's use of context. To gauge size, the visual system factors in how far away an object is judged to be, scaling the retinal image accordingly. [■] Color constancy works similarly, as the brain discounts the tint of the prevailing light so that a red apple appears red under both sunlight and lamplight. [■] Shape constancy, in turn, allows a door to seem rectangular even as it swings and casts a slanted trapezoid onto the retina. [■] These principles matter well beyond the laboratory. [■] Because the brain reads size and distance together, designers can manipulate one to suggest the other. Architects now apply these findings when they taper columns or narrow a corridor to make a hall feel longer and grander than its true dimensions.

questions.context: A suspension bridge is a structure whose deck hangs from cables strung between tall towers, letting it span gaps that no rigid beam could cross. The earliest versions were simple rope-and-plank walkways slung across gorges by ancient builders. Because their fibrous cables sagged and frayed, these crossings could carry only a few people at a time and rarely lasted more than a single season before rotting. Iron transformed this ancient idea into a form capable of bearing far heavier loads. [■] Nineteenth-century engineers replaced rope with wrought-iron chains, and later with spun steel wire, extending spans across wide rivers. [■] These early metal bridges swayed dangerously in strong winds, and several collapsed when gusts set their decks oscillating. [■] Modern designers solved the wind problem by studying how air flows around the deck. [■] Aerodynamic decks, which are shaped in wind tunnels, now let cables carry traffic across straits once thought impossible to bridge. Steel main cables, each bundling thousands of thin wires, distribute the deck's weight so evenly that a single span can now stretch for kilometers. However, even these refined structures face objections, since their enormous cost and vulnerability to earthquakes may leave the longest proposed crossings unbuilt.

questions.context: A Roman aqueduct was a channel engineered to carry water across great distances by means of a gentle, continuous downward slope. Fed by mountain springs, it delivered a steady flow to the fountains, baths, and households of distant cities. The whole system relied not on pumps but on gravity, which drew the water forward wherever the ground descended even slightly. This dependence on gravity forced surveyors to control the gradient with remarkable precision. [■] Over many kilometers the channel might drop only a few meters, so any error could halt the flow or let it overspill. Where valleys interrupted the route, engineers raised towering arcades of stone arches to keep the water at the proper height. [■] Where hills blocked the path, inverted siphons were used, sealed pipes that let pressure carry the water down one slope and up the next. [■] The finished aqueducts reshaped urban life by making abundant clean water ordinary rather than rare. [■] Public baths, latrines, and drinking fountains all depended on their unbroken supply. Researchers are continuously uncovering new branches and measuring the mineral deposits inside the channels to learn how much water these systems actually carried.

questions.context: A nebula is an immense cloud of gas and dust scattered through the space between the stars. Composed mainly of hydrogen, these clouds can stretch across many light-years yet remain thinner than any vacuum produced on Earth. Despite this near-emptiness, a nebula holds enough raw material to build thousands of stars. This star-building potential is released when part of a nebula grows dense enough to collapse under its own gravity. Such collapse is often triggered by a nearby shock wave, perhaps from an exploding star, that squeezes the gas past a critical density. As a clump contracts, it heats up and spins faster, eventually forming a hot core that will become a star. The surrounding gas, meanwhile, flattens into a rotating disk from which planets may later assemble. Astronomers study these regions by observing the light that passes through or radiates from the dust. Infrared telescopes are especially useful, because their wavelengths pierce the dusty veil that hides newborn stars from ordinary view. Together these observations reveal a nebula not as an empty smudge but as a nursery where gas, gravity, and time convert diffuse matter into stars and worlds.

questions.context: Water treatment is the set of chemical and physical processes that make raw water safe for people to drink. Water drawn from rivers and lakes carries suspended clay, dissolved minerals, and living microbes that simple straining cannot remove. Because the finest clay particles are far too small to be caught by an ordinary filter, cleaning such water demands a chemical step rather than a purely mechanical one. This chemical step, known as coagulation, solves the problem of tiny suspended particles. Treatment plants add coagulants such as aluminum salts, whose positive charges neutralize the negative charges that keep clay particles apart. Freed from this mutual repulsion, the particles collide and clump into larger clusters called flocs, which grow heavy enough to settle out of the water. These settled flocs are then filtered off, and the clarified water is disinfected to kill any remaining microbes. This combined approach has sharply reduced waterborne disease in cities that can afford it. Some dissolved chemicals still slip through, and disinfection itself may form unwanted byproducts, yet ongoing research into membranes and advanced oxidation promises even more sophisticated solutions.

options.C: How chemical treatment removes particles to make water drinkable

questions.context: Water treatment is the set of chemical and physical processes that make raw water safe for people to drink. Water drawn from rivers and lakes carries suspended clay, dissolved minerals, and living microbes that simple straining cannot remove. Because the finest clay particles are far too small to be caught by an ordinary filter, cleaning such water demands a chemical step rather than a purely mechanical one. This chemical step, known as coagulation, solves the problem of tiny suspended particles. Treatment plants add coagulants such as aluminum salts, whose positive charges neutralize the negative charges that keep clay particles apart. Freed from this mutual repulsion, the particles collide and clump into larger clusters called flocs, which grow heavy enough to settle out of the water. These settled flocs are then filtered off, and the clarified water is disinfected to kill any remaining microbes. This combined approach has sharply reduced waterborne disease in cities that can afford it. Some dissolved chemicals still slip through, and disinfection itself may form unwanted byproducts, yet ongoing research into membranes and advanced oxidation promises even more sophisticated solutions.
questions.stem: According to paragraph 3, what happens to the flocs after they form?

options.A: They are dissolved by adding still more coagulant
options.B: They are collected and later reused as fertilizer
options.C: They are heated until the water around them evaporates
options.D: They are filtered out before the water is disinfected

questions.subtopic: neuroplasticity and the brain's ability to rewire itself
questions.context: Neuroplasticity is the brain's ability to reorganize itself by forming new connections between its cells throughout life. For a long time, scientists assumed the adult brain was essentially fixed. Once you reached maturity, the thinking went, your wiring was set and could not change. We now know that view is wrong. The brain is constantly remodeling itself in response to what we do and experience. Let me define the key idea more precisely. Neurons communicate across tiny gaps called synapses. When two neurons fire together repeatedly, the connection between them strengthens; when a pathway goes unused, it gradually weakens. In other words, cells that fire together tend to wire together. Here's a striking example. Researchers studied London taxi drivers, who must memorize the city's thousands of streets. Brain scans revealed that a region called the hippocampus, which is involved in spatial memory, was noticeably larger in these drivers than in other people. And the longer someone had driven a taxi, the larger that region tended to be. Their brains had physically adapted to the demands of the job. This has real consequences. After a stroke damages part of the brain, patients can sometimes recover lost abilities because healthy regions take over the functions of the damaged area. Therapy that repeatedly exercises a weakened skill encourages the brain to rebuild those pathways. So the brain isn't a fixed machine you're stuck with. It's more like a living landscape, reshaped by everything you practice and repeat.
questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: The brain's ability to reorganize its connections throughout life
options.B: How London taxi drivers memorize thousands of city streets
options.C: How a stroke damages specific regions of the human brain
options.D: How neurons send signals across the gaps between them

questions.subtopic: how ice harvesting shaped nineteenth-century commerce
questions.context: Before mechanical refrigeration existed, how did people keep food cold in the summer? For much of the nineteenth century, the answer came from a surprising industry: natural ice harvesting. Ice cut from frozen lakes and rivers became a major commercial product, and it reshaped how people ate and traded. The business was pioneered by a New England merchant named Frederic Tudor, sometimes called the Ice King. In the early 1800s, Tudor had an unusual idea: cut ice from ponds in Massachusetts during winter and ship it to warm places that had never seen it. His first shipments to the Caribbean largely melted, and he lost money for years. The breakthrough came from better insulation. Workers learned that packing the ice in sawdust, a cheap waste product from lumber mills, dramatically slowed melting. With sawdust insulation, ice could survive long ocean voyages. Once the technique was proven, the trade exploded. Crews used horse-drawn cutters to score frozen lakes into a grid, then sawed out uniform blocks and stored them in insulated icehouses. Ice from New England was shipped as far as India, thousands of miles away, and much of it arrived intact. Closer to home, a network of icehouses and delivery wagons brought ice to city households and businesses. This cold chain changed daily life. Breweries could brew year-round, fresh fish and meat could travel farther, and ordinary families could store perishable food. So an industry built on frozen water helped lay the groundwork for the modern food economy, well before the electric refrigerator arrived.
questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How Frederic Tudor lost money on his first shipments
options.B: How the natural ice trade reshaped nineteenth-century commerce
options.C: How the electric refrigerator replaced older cooling methods
options.D: How sawdust was produced in New England lumber mills

Ice cut from frozen lakes and rivers became a major commercial product, and it reshaped how people ate and traded. The business was pioneered by a New England merchant named Frederic Tudor, sometimes called the Ice King. In the early 1800s, Tudor had an unusual idea: cut ice from ponds in Massachusetts during winter and ship it to warm places that had never seen it. His first shipments to the Caribbean largely melted, and he lost money for years. The breakthrough came from better insulation. Workers learned that packing the ice in sawdust, a cheap waste product from lumber mills, dramatically slowed melting. With sawdust insulation, ice could survive long ocean voyages. Once the technique was proven, the trade exploded. Crews used horse-drawn cutters to score frozen lakes into a grid, then sawed out uniform blocks and stored them in insulated icehouses. Ice from New England was shipped as far as India, thousands of miles away, and much of it arrived intact. Closer to home, a network of icehouses and delivery wagons brought ice to city households and businesses. This cold chain changed daily life. Breweries could brew year-round, fresh fish and meat could travel farther, and ordinary families could store perishable food. So an industry built on frozen water helped lay the groundwork for the modern food economy, well before the electric refrigerator arrived.
questions.stem: According to the professor, how far did New England ice travel?

options.A: Only to nearby towns within New England itself
options.B: Mostly to breweries along the Atlantic coast
options.C: Nowhere, because it always melted before arriving
options.D: As far as India, thousands of miles away

questions.subtopic: how mycorrhizal fungal networks let trees share resources underground
questions.context: When we look at a forest, we tend to see a collection of individual trees competing for light and water. But beneath the soil, something more cooperative is happening. Most trees are connected to one another through a vast underground web of fungi, and through that web they can actually share resources. Ecologists sometimes call this network the wood wide web. Here's how it works. The roots of most plants form a partnership with fungi called mycorrhizae. The fungus wraps around or grows into the roots and extends thread-like filaments far out into the soil. The tree supplies the fungus with sugars it makes through photosynthesis. In return, the fungus gathers water and nutrients like phosphorus and delivers them to the tree. It's a classic exchange that benefits both partners. But the fungal threads don't just link one tree to one fungus. They connect many trees together into a single network. Researchers have found that carbon can move through this network from one tree to another. In one famous experiment, an ecologist labeled the carbon in certain trees with a traceable isotope. She found that carbon flowed from trees growing in bright sunlight to shaded seedlings that couldn't make enough of their own. Larger, older trees often act as hubs, supporting younger ones nearby. This changes how we think about forests. A forest may function less like a crowd of rivals and more like an interconnected community, where the health of the whole depends on hidden partnerships we are only beginning to understand.
questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How a single fungus attaches itself to a tree's roots
options.B: How trees compete with one another for sunlight
options.C: How underground fungal networks let trees share resources
options.D: How forests are now mapped using internet technology

questions.subtopic: how sfumato created soft, hazy transitions in Renaissance painting
questions.context: Look closely at the Mona Lisa, and you'll notice something unusual about her face. There are no hard lines around the eyes or the corners of the mouth. Instead, the forms seem to melt gently into shadow. This soft, smoky quality has a name: sfumato. The word comes from the Italian fumo, meaning smoke, and it describes a technique that transformed Renaissance painting. Before sfumato, painters often defined shapes with clear, sharp outlines, almost like a drawing filled in with color. Leonardo da Vinci pioneered a different approach. Rather than drawing a boundary and coloring inside it, he built up form using extremely thin, translucent layers of paint called glazes. He would apply glaze after glaze, sometimes dozens of them, letting each one dry. The result was a gradual, almost imperceptible transition from light to dark, with no visible edge where one tone became another. Why did this matter so much? Consider how we actually see the world. In real life, edges are rarely crisp; objects blur slightly into their surroundings, especially in dim light or at a distance. By softening his transitions, Leonardo made his figures look more three-dimensional and lifelike than anything painted before. The haziness also lends his faces a sense of mystery, since an expression that never quite resolves invites the viewer to keep searching it. Sfumato became one of the most admired achievements of the High Renaissance. Later painters studied Leonardo's smoky shadows for generations, and the technique remains a benchmark for depicting the subtle play of light on the human face.
questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why the Mona Lisa's smile appears so mysterious
options.B: How Renaissance painters mixed their own paint colors
options.C: How smoke was depicted in Italian Renaissance paintings
options.D: A painting technique that softens transitions between light and shadow

- Enrollment is free and must be completed online by September 5.
- Commutes will be verified through the portal.
- Members who reach 100 points receive a $25 transit voucher.

Participants are reminded that bicycles must be registered at the Facilities Office before points can be earned. Questions may be directed to greencommute@campus.edu.
questions.stem: What can be inferred about a member who logs regular carpool trips all term?

options.A: They must pay for their own transit voucher
options.B: They cannot earn points without a bicycle
options.C: They could qualify for the transit voucher reward
options.D: They will lose points during holiday weeks
